votes.reason: The Python code correctly checks for the existence of two distinct indices whose elements sum to zero, matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly checks for the existence of two distinct indices whose elements sum to zero, which exactly matches the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly checks for the existence of two distinct indices i and j such that input[i] + input[j] == 0, which perfectly matches the Coq specification.

The Coq spec checks for existence of two distinct indices i, j (where i <> j) such that input[i] + input[j] = 0, and output = true iff such a pair exists. The Python code iterates through all pairs (i, j) with i < j, checking if any sum to zero. Since the Coq condition i <> j is symmetric (if i, j works, so does j, i), checking only i < j in Python is sufficient to detect the same pairs. Both return true when such a pair exists and false otherwise.

The Coq spec checks for existence of two distinct indices i, j (where i <> j) such that input[i] + input[j] = 0, and output = true iff such a pair exists. The Python code iterates through all pairs (i, j) with i < j, checking if any sum to zero. Since the Coq spec uses i <> j (not ordered), and addition is commutative, checking i < j in Python covers all unordered pairs. Both return true when such a pair exists and false otherwise, producing the same concrete results.

The Coq spec checks for existence of two distinct indices i, j (where i <> j) such that input[i] + input[j] = 0, and output = true iff such a pair exists. The Python code iterates through all pairs (i, j) with i < j, checking if any sum to zero. Since the Coq spec uses i <> j (not ordered), and addition is commutative, checking i < j in Python covers all unordered pairs. Both return true when such a pair exists and false otherwise, producing the same concrete results.
Python checks for any pair of distinct indices summing to zero (iterating i<j covers all) and returns whether output equals this existence, matching the Coq iff spec.
Python checks for any pair of distinct indices with elements summing to zero (using i<j) and enforces output equals that existence; this matches the Coq iff spec on all inputs.

Both define output=true iff there exist two distinct indices whose elements sum to zero. Python checks pairs with i<j (equivalent to i≠j) and returns output == has_pair, matching the Coq <-> relation. Precondition is also identical (True).
